data.title: Preferreds Weekly Review: Agency Mortgage REIT Results Support Preferreds
data.description:
Welcome to another installment of our Preferreds Market Weekly Review, where we discuss preferred stock and baby bond market activity from both the bottom-up, highlighting individual news and events, as well as top-down, providing an overview of the broader market. We also try to add some historical context as well as relevant themes that look to be driving markets or that investors ought to be mindful of. This update covers the period through the fourth week of October.

Be sure to check out our other weekly updates covering the business development company ("BDC") as well as the closed-end fund ("CEF") markets for perspectives across the broader income space.

Market Action

Preferreds were down around 1% on the week, along with most other income sectors. Rising Treasury yields have put pressure on the sector since the Fed cut the policy rate, causing the recent drop in preferreds yields to stall and partly reverse.

However, unlike in previous instances of rising yields, the market views higher yields as a sign of a resilient economy which, this time around, pushed spreads lower. Preferreds credit spreads stand near their lowest point in the last 5 years.

Market Themes

Agency mortgage REITs have started to report Q3 results. For preferreds shareholders there was much to like. Book value has been rising, leverage has been stable or falling while equity issuance has been coming at a healthy clip. All of this helps to derisk the preferreds by raising the equity / preferreds coverage ratio.

Our favored preferreds issuers in the sector DX, NLY, and AGNC in that order all have healthy levels of equity coverage.

All but two Agency mREIT preferreds have a SOFR-based Fix/Float coupon structure and of these we continue to like DX.PR.C which is expected to move out to the highest coupon level due to its elevated spread over SOFR.

Market Commentary

RiverNorth CEF (OPP) has launched a non-transferrable rights offering. What’s unusual about this rights offering is that it gives shareholders a right to buy common as well as preferred shares of the new preferred - the 6% 2029 maturity OPP.PR.C. OPP already has OPP.PR.A and OPP.PR.B trading with yields below 6% however these two are perpetual whereas OPP.PR.C will be a term preferred.

Shareholders will receive one right for each share held and 6 rights will be required to buy 2 new shares of the common stock and one share of OPP.PR.C. There will surely be a lot of OPP shareholders who don’t know a whole lot about preferreds and who won’t have a good sense of whether OPP.PR.C is good value or not.

At a 6% coupon, it would be the highest-yielding OPP preferred and the term feature makes it pretty attractive as a short-duration asset. For reference, OPP has modest leverage and a decent-quality portfolio with a bunch of Treasuries, non-Agency mortgages, CLO Debt, BDC bonds etc. There is no debt ahead of the preferreds in the capital structure. A 2% spread pick-up over Treasuries is not super exciting but it can certainly work as a dry powder allocation.

Very likely OPP.PR.C will be sold hard by some CEF investors who don’t understand it and, because duration is so short and liquidity is likely to be poor, even a small price drop will quickly boost the yield of the preferred. A $1 drop will add around 0.35% or so of yield to the preferred. Something to put on the watchlist.

data.title: M/I Homes: Riding The Housing Rebound With Strong Earnings And Growth Outlook
data.description:
M/I Homes (NYSE:MHO) stock has been on a superb run of late as the U.S. homebuilding space starts picking up the pace again. Fueled by recent interest rate cuts, real estate stocks like M/I Homes have enjoyed eye-catching gains, and with further rate cuts expected ahead, the rally will likely continue. Moreover, the company recently delivered another stellar quarterly result, posting record top-and-bottom-line numbers alongside robust operational activity. Supported by realistic growth assumptions from my sensitivity analysis and an appealing technical setup, M/I Homes stock has plenty of room to climb. Also, with favorable seasonality trends in November and December, now is an opportune time to buy.

Company Profile And Technical Setup

MI Homes has established its position as a true juggernaut in the U.S. homebuilding space since 1976, involved in designing and constructing single-family homes and townhomes built for various buyers, including millennials, first-timers, and multi-generational families. Headquartered in Columbus, Ohio, the company operates through three primary segments including Northern Homebuilding, Southern Homebuilding, and Financial Services. The Southern division accounts for nearly 60% of its total sales, generating $2.4 billion, while its Northern division contributes 37.8%, or $1.5 billion. Complementing its core construction operations, M/I’s Financial Services segment is responsible for $93.8 million, offering mortgage lending and title insurance to streamline home-buying.

Moreover, it boasts a market cap of $4.16 billion, employing over 1,600 people and constructing quality residences across Ohio, Indiana, Illinois, Michigan, Florida, Texas, North Carolina, and beyond. Also, with its end-to-end approach, from land acquisition and development to mortgage and title services, MI Homes has effectively positioned itself as a one-stop destination for homebuyers seeking efficiency and quality.

From the chart above, we can see the stock has shown strong upward momentum, as shown in two major bullish moves over the past few months. The first rally spanned over 15 weeks and posted a robust 58.17% gain, driven by market anticipation of rate cuts and strong earnings results. This was followed by a second upward move, lasting 10 weeks, with MHO stock climbing another 26.67%.

Currently trading at $150.23, MHO is positioned above a recent support level of $139.53, which has held firm despite the recent pullbacks. Additionally, this support level aligns with the 50-day EMA, underscoring its significance as a potential floor for the stock. If MHO can maintain its level, it could signify the continuation of a bullish trend, particularly if it can keep up the pace in the upcoming quarters.

On the RSI front, the indicator points to an easing from overbought levels, underscoring a period of consolidation rather than an immediate drop. A successful hold above $139.53 could offer a base for the next bullish leg, with $160 as a near-term target.

Q3 Earnings In Focus

MI Homes capped off another superb quarter with record highs in home deliveries, revenue, and income, proving resilient despite the macro-economic headwinds, including heightened interest rates and hurricane impacts. Impressively, Q3 marked their third straight quarter of top- and bottom-line outperformance, with solid YOY expansion across the board. Additionally, the company’s well-rounded product lineup and focus on expanding its community footprint point to a healthy outlook for the upcoming year.

The homebuilder posted an EPS of $5.10, topping estimates by 16 cents, complemented by an impressive $15.84 million sales beat.

Diving into the specifics, the company’s homebuilding revenue for the quarter climbed 3% YOY to roughly $1.11 billion, spearheaded by a solid 10% increase in housing sales. Still, a sizeable portion of those gains were balanced out by the drop in land sales. However, a remarkable 27% rise in financial services revenue to $29.37 million underscores its diversified revenue streams and strength in the mortgage space.

Furthermore, despite a 9% bump in the cost of sales, the company’s gross margin shot up a spectacular 10% to $309.44 million, highlighting its effective cost management and pricing strategy. Layered up with a 12% operating income boost to $181.93 million, the company’s net income rose to $145.43 million and a 6% gain in diluted EPS to $5.10.

Zooming out to review the nine months ended in September, MI Homes continued its remarkable run, with total homebuilding sales rising to $3.21 billion, an 8% increase on a YOY basis. Housing sales grew by 8% to $3.19 billion, while land revenue contracted by 50% to $11.75 million. Financial services revenue remained stellar, rising by 18% to $87.69 million. Moreover, its gross margin for the nine months jumped by a notable 16% to $902.06 million, while operating income grew by 13% to $542.10 million.

Furthermore, MI Homes ramped up its community footprint with a 7% YOY increase in average community count backed by a heartening 8% bump in home deliveries, hitting 2,271 units for the quarter and 6,653 year-to-date representing a 12% YOY rise. Additionally, the company’s average home closing price held steady at $489,000, underscoring robust pricing power and demand in a volatile market.

Moreover, during the earnings call, CEO Bob Schottenstein discussed MI Homes' resilience in navigating the current market headwinds, throwing weight around its robust financial position with zero borrowings under its credit facility and a healthy debt-to-capital ratio of 20%. Also, it ended the quarter with $2.8 billion in equity and a powerful cash balance of $720 million. What’s even more tremendous is that the company has grown its cash balance by nearly 7,000% from 2015.

Despite fluctuations in interest rates and disruptions from Hurricanes Helene and Milton, the company has seen encouraging signs in market demand, with traffic levels improving and October's performance exceeding expectations—setting a positive tone for Q4. Schottenstein expressed optimism for the upcoming spring selling season, even amidst market volatility and affordability pressures. To capture demand, MI Homes remains committed to strategic pricing across its diverse community portfolio, leveraging mortgage rate buy-downs and balancing its product offerings to appeal to both entry-level and move-up buyers​.

On top of that, Schottenstein talked about doubling down on affordable housing, with the company’s "Smart Series" now constituting a sizeable 20% of sales. The product line’s impact has evolved from surging demand from first-time and budget-conscious buyers, especially in booming markets like Florida and Texas. CEO Bob Schottenstein also highlighted that affordable townhomes in high-demand places such as Dallas and Orlando continue boosting sales and margins. Realtor.com’s October 2024 Housing Market Trends Report echoes this demand, noting a 34% rise in active listings throughout the South, with new listings up 2.9% while Tampa inventories rose an incredible 40.6% YOY. As inventory remains just below pre-pandemic levels, the demand for affordable housing in the South remains pivotal for M/I Home’s growth trajectory.

From the snapshot above, we can see that MI Homes is set for promising expansion ahead, with an expected 34.61% YOY surge in its Q4 EPS and positive momentum heading into 2025, as shown by a 4.90% YOY EPS growth by Q3 2025. The stock’s valuation appears highly attractive, trading at just 7.30 times forward earnings based on 2025 year-end estimates of $20.76 compared to its TTM PE ratio of 8.14 times.

Attractive At Current Levels

MI Homes stock has been on an excellent trajectory, posting an impressive 30% gain over the past six months, comfortably beating the S&P 500’s 14% gain over the same timeframe. However, we’ve seen a correction in the stock over the past month, losing almost 13% of its value amidst a volatile market. Nonetheless, the stock has more upside potential, supported by attractive valuation metrics and a sensitivity analysis grounded in realistic assumptions.

The snapshot shows that MI Homes stock is trading attractively based on a variety of metrics. It trades at 7.67 forward earnings, a significant 54.65% discount compared to the sector median of 16.92. Moreover, its EV/EBITDA sits at 6.54, roughly 42% lower than the sector median. Similarly, its price-to-book ratio is 1.47, offering a 31.20% discount relative to the sector’s 2.14 median.

Moreover, in gauging the stock’s fair value across various FCF growth scenarios and discount rates, I conducted a DCF analysis, building a sensitivity table highlighting substantial upside potential for MI Homes stock.

The analysis used a 10% FCF growth rate as a base case with a 12.4% discount rate in estimating potential fair values. The weighted-average-cost-of-capital (WACC) calculation incorporates an 81% equity-to-19% debt structure and a beta value of 1.82, underscoring the stock's volatility relative to the market. Keeping that in mind, here are the scenarios shown by the analysis.

Based on the analysis, we can see that MI Homes stock’s fair value is estimated at $179.51 based on a 10% FCF growth rate and a discount rate near its WACC of 12.4%. This represents a sizeable jump from its current stock price around the $150 mark. It is important to acknowledge, though, the stock’s rising WACC in recent years, peaking at 12.4% this year. As WACC increases, it becomes tough for companies to generate returns exceeding their cost of capital.

Risks To The Thesis

While M/I Homes is on an encouraging trajectory, multiple factors could weigh down the bullishness. Firstly, indecisiveness from the Federal Reserve, marked by shifts in their rate policy, could lead to an uncertain economic environment, impacting buyer confidence in the housing industry. Additionally, M/I’s exposure to the Southern U.S., though currently advantageous, could potentially become a liability on the back of economic setbacks or regulatory roadblocks. Also, M/I’s expansion into affordable housing, though appealing, heightens its competition with both new and established players, eating into its already meager margins.

Finally, with a WACC, M/I Homes stock remains vulnerable to market volatility. The recent uptick in WACC underscores a growing hurdle in generating returns exceeding the company’s cost of capital.

Takeaway On MI Homes stock

M/I Homes stock still has room to run, supported by healthy fundamentals, an appealing valuation, and its unique positioning in high-demand markets. With record-breaking quarterly numbers in Q3 and a diversified revenue model, the company is in an excellent position to sustain growth. Its attractive valuation metrics and end-to-end business model solidify its appeal. Moreover, favorable seasonality and technical indicators underscore strong near-term momentum, while the long-term growth prospects remain underpinned by growing community footprints and demand for affordable housing.

data.title: Saab: The ADR Isn't Reflective Of The Native Here Due To The Split
data.description:
Dear readers/followers,

Saab (OTCPK:SAABF) (OTCPK:SAABY) is one of the premiere Swedish defense stocks - and a stock that I was invested in at one time, but no longer am at this particular time. This has to do with valuation. A look at my last article, which you can find here, implies that the company is up 400% in less than a year. While this would be excellent (and while the ADR indeed has done this due to the stock split made by SAAB), this is not reflective of a potential native investment, which is up around 38% in the same timeframe accounting for the split. While this is great, it's not as market-beating as the 400% implied here.

It's an excellent example of the disconnect that can sometimes be between an ADR and a native share. In this article, I will be updating SAAB and see what the company can provide us with. This is a great defense stock and a qualitative company with an excellent history - but I will be clear about the fact that I don't believe it is worth as much as this, and looking at the native share, the company has gone nowhere in 6 months.

In this article, I'll update SAAB AB and see where the company could go from here. While I do want to own SAAB, I will never do so at any valuation - and this is one of those times where I would be quite careful.

SAAB - Updating for the latest quarters and looking at 2024-2026E.

SAAB AB, trading at native SAAB-B in Stockholm, is a BBB+ rated, sub-21% long-term debt/capital-leveraged aerospace and defense company. It comes at a market cap of roughly $10.5B and despite its comparatively smaller size, it works with some of the most storied products and subsidiaries in the European defense industry, including Kockums, which is a part of SAAB.

SAAB's yield is extremely meager - less than 0.75% - so this is a capital appreciation story more than it in any way is an income story. Furthermore, the company has a horrible track record of hitting targets.

Enter the Russo-Ukraine war, the event that brought stability to SAAB. I won't say "saved" SAAB because SAAB was never in any danger of bankruptcy, but it had been "sloughing" along the same trajectory for over a decade, not really doing anything impressive for its shareholders. That's why most investors I know avoided this one as a component of an investment portfolio.

This of course changed when the war came. I belong to the group of investors that preferred non-Swedish companies, such as Rheinmetall (OTCPK:RNMBY), BAE Group (OTCPK:BAESY), Rolls-Royce (OTCPK:RYCEF), and others.

As such, by the time it was time to look at SAAB as an investment, the company had already gone up so much that I didn't view it as investable any longer. This, in retrospect, was a mistake.

Now SAAB is caught in the same defense/aerospace frenzy as most of Europe and in some parts the US defense sector. Forecasts are guiding for double-digit, 20%+ annualized EPS growth on the back of extreme interest in defense spending. Some of it is true and proven, while part of it, at least in my view, is based more on speculation than anything else. That's the part where the companies "lose" me.

For SAAB, we have 3Q24 results that at this point are less than 10 days old. For 3Q24, SAAB saw exactly the same sort of positive/great trends that other companies in the same field saw and were under, including Rheinmetall, which I reviewed quite recently. For SAAB, we have a sharp increase of 40%+ in orders, 17% in sales, 38% in EBIT, and OCF, which is up significantly from a negative YoY level. Again, this highlights that for a long time, SAAB was a negative OCF company. It's only now that this is no longer the case, and it begs the question of how long this can last.

In my earlier articles on SAAB, I made it clear that the company is a 5-business area company with a focus on Aeronautics (like the Gripen and other planes), Dynamics (Ammo and other things), Surveillance (tech and systems, some GlobalEye), Kockums (Submarines and Naval tech, including combat Boats), as well as the Combitech segment, which focuses on digitalization.

Like any company, SAAB is also going through an efficiency program, which for the quarter has focused on Norwegian divestments and productivity improvements. Also, SAAB is significantly expanding its US exposure. The company is taking advantage of the increased traction in the field to open new production capacities in munition and has also been selected by the U.S. Army for the Individual Assault Munition program (The AT4 Solution by SAAB.). The US Air Force has also picked the Carl Gustaf System, which further increases interest by SAAB to expand to the US, and that's before even mentioning the SAAB tech accelerator Skapa (which means 'create'), a US AI modeling contract, which obviously also brings interest.

In short, the USA is a growing market here - and this is perhaps the strongest positive that I see for SAAB here.

SAAB has now grown to over 1,000 US-based employees and has generated US-based sales growth of over 53% in the last 9-month YoY period.

In terms of the various individual segments, things are actually not looking rosy in a way that would justify massive growth. This is likely the reason why the upside and growth are not as massive as you might have expected, and why the company actually dropped following its earnings. Kockums saw flat development in margins and a slight interest in sales. Dynamics was also a sales growth segment, but an EBIT margin decline segment.

The overall picture that SAAB gives after 3Q is that top-line interest and growth trends are very solid, but the bottom-line margins are not holding up quite as well across several segments. The focus is on orders and sales, rather than efficiencies. Part of this is understandable and okay - the company is growing, both in demand and production base. However, it does mean that all that EPS growth that's being forecasted might not be as certain as the forecasts or the bullish investors would have you believe - simply because the margins for all that sales/order volume won't hold up in a way that would generate that bottom-line growth.

And I believe the uncertainty will remain because SAAB has been very clear that it intends to focus on capacity and production ramp-ups, industrialization and efficiencies, and digitization as well as R&D investments, workforce investments, and mitigating SCM risks. All of this will cost money - which SAAB will have in spades with these order trends - but it also means that I don't consider the forecasts for EPS all that likely.

Please do take the risks and negatives I say here with more than a "spoonful" of salt, however - this is a great company in the midst of the defense/aerospace demand upcycle. I believe things will be positive for SAAB, and 3Q24 was a good quarter. It's just that the interest in the company, much like with other defense contractors, has peaked in a way that I view as unhealthy.

I spoke about it in my Rheinmetall review. The market has completely disconnected from the risk that the war in Ukraine might actually calm down, and that the geopolitical situation may become more stable, not less. Either no one believes this is possible, or they are falsely (in my opinion) accounting for that valuation risk.

And that's not something I am prepared to do.

Valuation for SAAB is so-so - I don't see a significant upside, and that means I avoid it

As I said earlier, SAAB has already declined from its "highs" here. I'm talking about a native move from 272 SEK to 220 SEK, where it is currently. This is a step in the right direction. But the valuation is still "so-so". In my last article, I made a case for investing at 420 SEK - but this was prior to a split, which the company did in the early summer of this year. The company has, since its peak, declined by around 20%.

Adjusted for the split, my PT for the company at a conservative level comes to around 185 SEK for 2025E, and including some of the upside which I consider to be likely. That means that the company could become interesting if it dropped below 200 SEK.

SAAB typically, over the 20-year average, trades at 18-19x P/E. For this company, and including the recent surge in defense spending, I am prepared to accept 20x P/E as well for the long term. But not 25-29x P/E, or 28.5x P/E as the company trades at today. I view this as inflated and temporary. SAAB will not continue to grow at 20-40% EPS growth annually, nor will it with a high likelihood even continue to do so in the next 2-4 years (again, my view).

At 18-20x P/E, at this price of 220 SEK, the company comes to a normalized annual upside of around 4.4% at the mid-point of 19x, and just above 5.5% at 20x P/E. Obviously, since I want 15% annually, that's not good enough for me. That's also the reasoning and logic behind my valuation targets - I want a 15% annualized upside, and I'm not getting that here.

I would get that, using these targets, at a share price of around 185 SEK, and then it would be possible even at a lower growth rate than this. So I hope we see a further decline here in the near term because that would enable me to start adding SAAB to my portfolio - which is something I have been "hoping" to do for a long time.

My thesis for the company at this time is as follows.

Thesis

  • Saab is Sweden's largest defense contractor, and a storied part of our history, constructing some of the best military products Sweden offers. I view it as an excellent company with a great upside - if you buy it at the right price.
  • The right price, in my opinion, is somewhere along the 185 SEK mark, with this being the maximum acceptable PT- and the company is currently far from this. There are methods for investing where we could go into Saab, but these methods, such as buy-writes, do not currently offer the sort of annualized return profile that would set them apart from lower-risk investments with better yields.
  • For that reason, I consider Saab to be a weak prospect here, and I would say that it warrants no more than a "HOLD" here. My PT goes to 185, and I don't go above that for 2024E either.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside that is high enough, based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators, but 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. Based on this, and a valuation target of 185 SEK/share where I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me, I give the company a "Hold" rating as of November 2024. The company is neither cheap or with an upside at this particular valuation.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: NextEra Energy's Backlog Is Set For AI Growth
data.description:
NextEra Energy (NYSE:NEE) has a strong growth trajectory as it navigates through an environment of accelerating demand for power. Hyperscalers will likely provide significant capacity growth later in the decade, whether it’s through grid connections or with dedicated baseload capacity. Near-term demand may impact rates as industry competes to secure power and new capacity is being added. Given the accelerated growth trajectory and drive for decarbonization by the hyperscalers, I am reiterating my BUY rating for NEE shares with a price target of $93/share at 16x eFY25 EV/aEBITDA.

Be sure to read my initial coverage of NextEra Energy Here:

NextEra Energy Is The Slow Growth Green Utility To Own

NextEra Energy Operation

NextEra is finding itself in a strong position as demand for renewable energy generation and storage remains strong. Management reported adding 3GW on power to its backlog in q3’24, bringing the trailing twelve-month total to 11GW. This figure is exclusive of the agreements with two Fortune 50 companies outside of the technology sector. The agreements are for the development of 10.5GW of renewable power generation and storage projects through 2030. This will bring the total to 15GW of joint developments inclusive of the 4.5GW with Entergy announced in q2’24. The total backlog at NEER now sits at 24GW.

Much of the growth will be driven by the growing data center footprint, which is forecast to grow at a 23% CAGR through 2030. For example, Oracle Corp. (ORCL) is planning to construct 100 data centers, some of which will be 200MW in capacity and upwards of 1GW in capacity or about the size of a small city. Digital Realty (DLR), a data center REIT, is bringing 390MW of capacity online throughout eFY25.

Microsoft (MSFT) has earmarked some of its facilities to be powered by nuclear power as well as dedicated small modular reactors (“SMRs”) through its partnership with Helion Energy. Given that the hyperscalers are hyper-focused on managing down their carbon footprint, I’m anticipating many of these facilities to follow suit. I have reason to believe that many, if not most, of these large-scale data centers will also seek dedicated power sources given the current power constraints faced. Though this is an exciting development for the utility space, new capacity will likely not hit the market until 2030-2035 and beyond.

Management at NextEra anticipates power and storage demand to grow by 150GW through 2030 as a result of the data center build-out. They also suggested that nuclear capacity will grow by 900GW on a national level by 2040. Management is currently evaluating the recommissioning of their Duane Arnold nuclear plant in Iowa to cater to this demand.

Given the growing demand and scarcity of power, NextEra may be in a prime position to cater to this need. Assessing the verbiage in the q3’24 earnings call, I believe that NextEra might be in a position to lock in power supply at a premium to market for dedicated usage. This can potentially play into the growing demand from the hyperscalers as these data centers are central to their operations and require zero downtime. Depending on how deals are worked out, I believe that constructing dedicated baseload capacity can be an accretive to NextEra’s financial position.

Looking at solar, wind, and storage, NextEra added 3GW of capacity to its backlog in q3’24. Management anticipates new renewables capacity will be an impactful solution when compared to new gas capacity and suggests that wind and solar are upwards of 60% and 40% cheaper than new gas capacity. Management suggested that renewables growth will increase by 3x in the next 7 years when compared to the growth seen in the last 7 years. In terms of storage, management expects 50GW of co-located capacity to be needed through 2027.

As a result of the 2024 hurricane season, management anticipates that FPL will recover $1.2b from customers through a surcharge while allocating $150mm of the surcharge to the storm reserve.

NextEra Financial Position

Looking to NextEra’s financial position, the firm realized significant margin improvement across its earning assets, resulting in an adjusted net margin of 28% and an adjusted EPS of $1.03/share. Looking out to eq4’24, I’m forecasting NextEra to generate $7,150mm in total revenue with an adjusted EPS of $0.55/share. This should bring eFY24 revenue to $26.5b and its adjusted EPS to $3.45/share. Given the strong demand for securing power as well as the growing demand by the hyperscalers, I have reason to believe NextEra will be in a strong position and may have the capacity to grow beyond its guided growth trajectory of 6-8% compounded growth from 2024 through 2027.

NextEra’s debt position is running relatively high; however, I do not believe this will pose any headwinds. The firm has no maturities coming due until 2026.

Risks Related To NextEra Energy

Bull Case

Electricity is growing in demand is AI factories are rolled out around the world. This has resulted in the recommissioning of nuclear power plants as well as discussions between hyperscalers and utilities for co-development projects for dedicated baseload capacity. Given that the hyperscalers are seeking low-carbon sources, NextEra is well positioned to cater to this demand.

If the Federal Reserve were to continue cutting rates going forward, NextEra may benefit in terms of refinancing debt or deploying new capital for new projects.

Bear Case

Many of the potential large-scale co-development projects will likely not hit NextEra's operating statements until later in the decade or in the mid-2030s as SMR technology has yet to be utilized in the US. NextEra is also running up its debt load relative to adjusted EBITDA; however, the firm does not have any maturities through 2026 that will need to be refinanced or paid down. If the Fed maintains the current key rate, the cost of capital may impact the firm's average interest rate if the need to raise capital were to arise.

Valuation & Shareholder Value

NEE shares provide investors a strong investment opportunity for both its dividend and growth opportunity. The shares currently yield 2.60% on a forward basis, providing investors with a durable source of income. Management anticipates growing the dividend rate by 10% per year through at least 2026.

Looking to NEE’s valuation, shares are currently trading just above the midpoint of its 1-year trading range at 15.76x EV/aEBITDA. Taking into consideration my growth forecast as well as its historical trading range, I am reiterating my BUY rating for NEE shares with a price target of $93/share at 16x eFY25 EV/aEBITDA.

data.title: CEF Weekly Review: Invesco Has A Do-Over
data.description:
Welcome to another installment of our CEF Market Weekly Review, where we discuss closed-end fund ("CEF") market activity from both the bottom-up – highlighting individual fund news and events – as well as the top-down – providing an overview of the broader market. We also try to provide some historical context as well as the relevant themes that look to be driving markets or that investors ought to be mindful of.

This update covers the period through the fourth week of October. Be sure to check out our other weekly updates covering the business development company ("BDC") as well as the preferreds/baby bond markets for perspectives across the broader income space.

Market Action

All CEF sector NAVs were down this week save for the loan sector which was flat. Discounts were mixed. Through October, CEFs are mostly down with the exception of MLPs.

Fixed-income CEF sector discounts have come off a tad off a fairly tight level while equity CEF sector discounts have remained stable over the last few weeks.

Market Themes

Invesco is liquidating the CMBS CEF IHTA in Dec-2024. This has been expected for a long while and investors can remember the liquidation of the sister fund IHIT a year before. In the 6 months prior to termination, IHTA NAV had a 6% drop - much better than the 15% drop for IHTA.

The tightening in the discount offset the drop in the IHTA NAV this year so unlike for IHIT there were modest gains to be had in IHTA in a termination strategy. The strength in the CMBS market and good overall market liquidity surely helped IHTA preserve value in winding down its portfolio. Apart from this pair of funds, term CEFs have generally seen very stable NAV performance into the termination.

Market Commentary

A RiverNorth CEF (OPP) has launched a non-transferrable rights offering. What’s unusual about this rights offering is that it gives shareholders a right to buy common as well as shares of the new preferred - the 6% 2029 maturity OPP.PR.C. OPP already has OPP.PR.A and OPP.PR.B trading with yields below 6% however these two are perpetual whereas OPP.PR.C will be a term preferred. Shareholders will receive one right for each share held and 6 rights will be required to buy 2 new shares of the common stock and one share of OPP.PR.C.

A new CLO Equity CEF started trading - the Pearl Diver Credit Company (PDCC). NAV distribution rate is 13.2% and the premium is 1%. The yield is on the lower end of CLO Equity CEFs but similar to SPMC which went public recently and was highlighted. The one key difference with SPMC is that PDCC is a new fund so it doesn’t have much of a track record whereas SPMC had a strong one as a previously private fund. The difficulty with allocating to new CLO Equity funds with no track record is that performance is highly divergent - the best has delivered a total NAV 15% CAGR over the last 5 years and the worst 3.8%.

The Flaherty suite of preferreds funds raised their distributions once again, this time by 2-3%. This is the fourth raise over the past year for a total of around 12%. The raises have been pretty regular - one raise per quarter.

The funds have not yet benefited from the cut in leverage costs but that will over the coming months. The Flaherty suite of preferreds funds is the only reasonably valued one in the suite at mid-single-digit discounts - all the other preferred CEFs are pretty rich save for NPFD which is a higher-quality, higher-fee fund i.e., one with a likely lower level of net investment income.

data.title: VICI Is The Highest Quality REIT With Ample Upside
data.description:
In this article we will first examine VICI's (NYSE:VICI) latest earnings to then analyze what sets them apart and what might be some risks to consider. After this we will evaluate the company using a Discount Cash Flow Analysis (DCF). Within our most optimistic scenario we get a potential undervaluation of up to 54%.

Q3/2024 Earnings

With total revenues up 6.7% year over year to $964.7 million and net income attributable to stockholders up 31.7% to $732.9 million, VICI Properties had a successful third quarter of 2024. At $593.9 million, Adjusted Funds from Operations (AFFO) increased 8.4%. The company announced a 4.2% increase in its quarterly cash dividend of $0.4325 per share. At the conclusion of the quarter, VICI had $355.7 million in cash on hand and had deployed $230 million in capital through loan and growth fund agreements.

The remarks made by CEO Edward Pitoniak demonstrate VICI Properties' effective growth strategy, which emphasizes consistent dividend increases and sustained revenue. The company's capital deployment, which is mostly made up of loans and growth fund investments, shows a targeted allocation meant to support tenant growth and strengthen the company's portfolio. The CEO also highlights VICI's 100% rent collection record, implying that a solid basis for compounding growth is provided by steady tenant dependability and substantial secular tailwinds.

With encouraging medium- to long-term possibilities, VICI Properties seems to be carrying out a methodical, growth-oriented approach. The CEO's remarks demonstrate a thorough awareness of the business's advantages, particularly its steady revenue streams and calculated expenditures meant to promote long-term growth. VICI's robust financial indicators put it in a good position to take advantage of favorable trends in the gaming and leisure industries, strengthen its capital position, and strengthen its relationships with tenants. Thus, the business's performance and strategic orientation indicate a promising future for resilience and expansion.

What Sets Them Apart

VICI Properties distinguishes itself from other triple-net lease REITs in a number of ways:

  • Occupant Business Model: While traditional REITs often lease to traditional products and services tenants, VICI focuses on experiential, operational, and revenue-diverse real estate linked to high-demand industries like gaming and entertainment.

  • High Barriers to Entry: VICI has a competitive edge because of its properties, which are frequently located in desirable areas like the Las Vegas Strip. All the blue properties are currently owned by VICI.

  • Large Average Rent per Asset: VICI has a high-value, high-revenue portfolio, with an average rent per asset of over $32.9 million, which is significantly higher than the ~$400,000 typical of other triple-net REITs.

  • Non-Commoditized Real Estate: Unlike standard REITs, which sometimes deal in commoditized, interchangeable assets, VICI's properties are distinctive and unusual.

  • Long Remaining Lease Term: VICI's properties offer consistent, predictable income because their average remaining lease term is about 41 years, as opposed to 8–14 years for other REITs.

  • Stable Rent Collection and Minimal Cash Flow fluctuation: VICI's 100% rent collection rate and minimal cash flow fluctuation highlight the tenants' sound financial standing.

  • CPI-Protected Rent Roll and Steady Rent increase: VICI's long-term leases include a 1.7% same-store rent increase rate and 90% CPI (Consumer Price Index) protection, which both safeguard revenue from inflation and provide steady income growth.

All this while having a solid balance sheet and debt that is nicely evened out over the next few years.

This can also be seen when we compare Vici to the major competitors Iron Mountain (IRM), Realty Income (O) Gaming and Leisure Properties (GLPI) and EPR Properties (EPR), where VICI is currently valuated attractively while also having one of the highest growth rates.

Valuation - Discounted Cash Flow Analysis

For our analysis we need a few main assumptions. These will stay the same, while we assume different revenue growth rates for a Bear-, Base- and Bull-Case scenario.

  • FFO Margin & Free Cash Flow Conversion: Here I averaged out the last four years and used 61.8% FFO margin and 114% FFO to FCF conversion.
  • WACC: Here I assumed 8%, which is a higher and therefore more conservative compared to VICI's current WACC of 7.25%.
  • Perpetuity Growth Rate: Considering the above-mentioned guaranteed 1.7% same-store rent increase and the CPI protection, 3% seems fitting here.

Bear-Case:

For the Bear Case I assumed that Vici only managed to grow top line by 2.5% p.a. for the next 8 years. Like mentioned above this seems very conservative considering the guaranteed 1.7% same-store rent increase and the CPI protection.

Even with these very pessimistic assumptions, we get a fair value share price of $33, indicating that the company could nevertheless be undervalued by 4%.

Base-Case

A growth rate of 7% p.a. for our Base Case scenario seems also very achievable for VICI considering historical AFFO and FFO growth rates.

The historical growth of VICI's FFO per share - so adjusted for capital increases - is between 8.2% (3 year) and 16.4% p.a. (5 year) and their declared growth rate in this quarter was at 6.7%, also indicating that 7% p.a. should be achievable in the future.

With these realistic assumptions, we get a fair value share price of $49, indicating that the company is currently undervalued by 36%.

Bull-Case

For the Bull-Case we assumed a growth rate of 11% p.a., this seems still doable for Vici considering the historical growth rates mentioned above and the aggressive acquisitions strategy over the last years.

Within our Bull-Case we get a fair value share price of $69 (nice), indicating that the company could be overvalued by up to 54%.

Risks To Consider

Like always there are however some risks to consider:

  • High Tenant Concentration: Caesars Entertainment and MGM Resorts are only two of the primary tenants that VICI mostly depends on. Due to this concentration, if one of these important tenants experiences defaults or financial difficulties, VICI's revenue may be severely impacted.

  • Specialized, Concentrated Business Model: Diversification is constrained by VICI's emphasis on gaming and experience properties. Although this specialization may be advantageous in prosperous times, it increases the company's susceptibility to downturns in the business, changes in regulations, or changes in customer demand in the gambling and hotel industries.
  • Economic Sensitivity: Discretionary expenditure is a key component of VICI's portfolio. Gaming and entertainment revenues may suffer from a recession or a decline in consumer spending, which might therefore have an effect on VICI's rental income and occupancy rates.
  • Regulatory Risks: VICI is vulnerable to regulatory risks since a large number of its assets are in the casino industry. Stricter rules or modifications to gaming legislation may have an impact on tenant operations and cause variations in income.

Conclusion

With our DCFs we get the following valuations:

Bear: $33 - 4% undervaluation

Base: $49 - 36% undervaluation

Bull: $69 - 54% undervaluation

With this in mind and despite the mentioned risks to the company, I am currently rating the company a Strong Buy and I am expanding my position.

What’s Your Perspective? Let’s Discuss!

Thank you for reading my analysis! I'd love to hear your ideas and points of view - whether you agree, disagree, or have your own take on the stock. Let's start a discussion in the comments! I welcome any helpful arguments and ideas that can help us better comprehend this topic. Thanks!

~ Fabian - FF

data.title: Linde: Dragged Down By Slowdown In Asia And Europe
data.description:
Linde (NASDAQ:LIN) is one of the strongest businesses that we know of.

Linde, a supplier of industrial gases, delivers over 25% in after-tax returns and has recently achieved annual earnings-per-share growth exceeding 10%.

Such impressive returns on capital are highly unusual, particularly given the stability provided by its long-term contracts that allow it to pass on energy costs and inflation. In essence, Linde operates much like an industrial utility, with steady earnings and minimal volatility.

Linde should be on everyone’s radar. Exceptional businesses like this rarely come at clear bargain prices, but purchasing at reasonable levels can offer attractive returns over the long term. We keep it on our reserve list waiting for a chance to buy.

However, Linde is currently trading at around 30X PE ratio, which is at the higher end of its historical valuation range. This premium valuation comes as the economic environment overseas shows signs of weakening, potentially adding headwinds for growth in international markets.

This might be the wrong time to invest in this high-quality business.

Linde has had an impressive run, but starting to slow

Linde is a leading global industrial gases and engineering company, supplying gases like oxygen, hydrogen, and carbon dioxide for various industrial processes, including steel manufacturing and beverage carbonation. The company also has a significant medical division, providing oxygen and other gases to hospitals and home care patients.

In more cyclical markets like commodity production, Linde secures long-term take-or-pay contracts that include energy cost pass-through clauses. This structure means Linde would only lose revenue if customers went bankrupt. In less cyclical industries, such as food and beverage, demand remains more stable. Linde has also been able to pass through inflation either through contractual provisions or management price/mix initiatives.

This business can continue to grow even in challenging economic conditions, though at a slower rate. A prolonged stagnation, similar to Japan’s economic scenario, would likely be the worst-case situation for Linde. However, this is unlikely to pose a significant risk, as Linde operates globally and across diverse end markets.

Over the past four years, Linde has demonstrated remarkable financial performance. As inflation rose in North America and Europe, the company effectively passed on cost increases to customers while maintaining flat operating expenses. This strategic approach led to a 60% increase in operating profits during this period.

As profits raced ahead, so did the share price. Linde is currently selling at ~30X forward earnings, an upper bound of its longer-term valuation range. Having said it, the growth prospects are not at multiyear best levels.

The current macroeconomic environment presents challenges. Inflation has stabilized at lower levels in Western countries, and China is experiencing deflationary trends. Additionally, industrial output in Europe and Asia is stagnant, limiting same-store volume growth potential.

This could be the wrong time to buy this high-quality business, having said it, these headwinds might soon give us an opportunity to buy it at a more favourable price. It’s time to keep our eyes peeled and wait for an opportunity.

Recent financial performance

Linde reported their third-quarter earnings on 31st October and has cautioned investors about the ongoing weakness in EMEA and Asian markets. While America has stayed resilient, Chinese stagnation is continuing as the previously announced stimulus measures are not being felt yet. The management has also cautioned that demand for large-ticket consumer discretionary items, such as cars, is slowing and hurting industrial activity globally.

The volume has stayed stagnant during this quarter as compared to the same period last year. The management of Linde has managed to pass on cost increase as compared to a year ago, but price performance has stayed broadly flat as compared to the previous quarter.

In response to this darkening outlook, the management is doubling down on cost-cutting efforts to maintain profit margins even if top-line trends become more challenging. The management has pointed out that they see no prospects for improvement in China, especially given the current political backdrop.

Linde has broadly reiterated its full-year earnings growth, but the risk for more adverse performance further out seems to be growing.

Valuation and financials

Most analysts expect Linde to continue growing earnings per share at about 10% per annum over the next few years. This might be difficult to do if the current weakness in international markets continues. Dollar strength could also be a headwind on reported earnings.

Linde has laid out quite a transparent earnings growth algorithm for its investors over the past few years:

  • 1-2% EPS growth from backlog start-ups;
  • 4-6% EPS growth from pricing and productivity;
  • 2% EPS growth from share buybacks.

The order backlog continues to be strong mainly due to ongoing strength in the U.S. and share buybacks do continue. The main potential issue is with the pricing and productivity line.

Over the past 4 years, Linde passed on a cumulative of ~20% price increases while keeping its operating costs stable. The operating profit increased by ~$3+ billion as a result. This growth was achieved with very little volume improvement.

During this period Linde leveraged the price increases due to operating cost discipline and was able to grow earnings considerably. If it is unable to pass on price increases at the same level as before, growing earnings will become a lot more difficult.

Linde, $ million

Linde financials, our estimates

Securing price increases may become increasingly difficult amid a backdrop of potentially declining global industrial output and a generally stagnating Chinese economy. Additionally, the low inflation environment in the U.S. could make it harder to implement price hikes domestically.

Macroeconomic headwinds could lead investors to question Linde’s ability to achieve its targeted EPS growth in the coming quarters.

We remain confident that Linde will continue to grow its earnings, but we are increasingly concerned about the pace of this growth. The company is trading 30X forward PE, at the high end of its historic valuation range, but the growth is becoming more difficult to achieve.

Given the current macroeconomic environment, we believe the stock could adjust to its 5-year median P/E multiple of 27X. This would imply a merely 10% price correction and it is no reason to sell this high-quality stock.

Having said this, we do not currently see much upside in the stock. We Hold.

The risks

  • The Chinese economy could recover unexpectedly, also reinvigorating European exporters. Recovering industrial activity and stronger inflation levels in Asia and EMEA would give an earnings boost to Linde and might cause the stock price to appreciate considerably.
  • Given the low-risk nature of the business, Linde equity could be seen as a bond. As interest rates decline, bond prices rise. In a falling interest rate environment the PE multiple of Linde could potentially increase even further.
